what_i_noticed: Screener endpoint loops 30+ stocks sequentially (90-240s total). No request timing observability. GDELT makes 3 sequential HTTP calls with no retry. All hardcoded values not in config.py.
hypothesis: Parallelizing the screener with ThreadPoolExecutor (8 workers) will reduce latency 3-5x. Adding request timing middleware will enable future optimization. Parallelizing GDELT + adding retry will improve news reliability.
what_i_did: 1. Created backend/middleware.py with TimingMiddleware (X-Process-Time header, slow request logging). 2. Parallelized screener stock analysis loop with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8). 3. Parallelized sector momentum ETF fetches (6 workers). 4. Parallelized GDELT 3 HTTP calls + added @retry_with_backoff(max_retries=2). 5. Moved all hardcoded performance values to config['performance'] section. 6. Removed unused import. 7. Wrote 28 new tests covering middleware, parallel patterns, GDELT edge cases, config validation.

after.request_observability: X-Process-Time header + slow request warning logs

analysis: The parallelization is sound but NOT validated end-to-end under load. The estimated speedup (3-5x) assumes yfinance doesn't internally serialize requests — yf.Ticker().history() creates per-object sessions so parallel should be safe, but I haven't profiled in production. The middleware is lightweight (<1ms overhead). The GDELT parallelization is the cleanest win — 3 independent HTTP calls running in parallel is unambiguously better. The broad except in _analyze_one (screener) is intentionally kept — it's the outer safety net for per-ticker failures in a batch operation.
what_i_would_do_differently: 1. Should have written a slow integration test that actually measures screener latency before/after with real network calls (would need @pytest.mark.slow). 2. The screener parallelism doesn't address the deeper issue: _compute_market_signal() is called ONCE before the parallel loop, but _analyze_stock (single-ticker endpoint) calls it again + calls _compute_sector_momentum() redundantly. A caching layer for market_signal with 5-min TTL would help more. 3. I didn't narrow the broad excepts in portfolio_engine.py and stock_analyzer.py — left for a future cycle as it requires understanding each exception's semantics.
next_steps: 1. Add @cached(ttl=300) to _compute_market_signal() so repeated single-stock requests don't re-fetch market data. 2. Narrow broad excepts in portfolio_engine.py (5) and stock_analyzer.py (8). 3. Add structured JSON logging with request IDs for correlation. 4. Profile actual screener latency in production to validate the 3-5x hypothesis.
